lesson_title: Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.text: A {{c1::smart pointer}} is a composition class that manages {{c2::dynamically allocated memory}} and ensures it gets deleted when the smart pointer goes {{c3::out of scope}}.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.front: Why was std::auto_ptr deprecated in C++11?
cards.back: std::auto_ptr implements move semantics using copy semantics, which causes the source object to be modified during a copy. This violates the principle that copy operations shouldn't modify the source object.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.question: What's the problem with this code using std::auto_ptr?
cards.answer: res1 is now null after the copy to res2. The copy operation actually moved ownership, so dereferencing res1 causes undefined behavior.
cards.explanation: std::auto_ptr implements move semantics through copy operations, so copying transfers ownership and nullifies the source pointer.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.text: {{c1::Move semantics}} means a class will transfer ownership of an object's resources rather than {{c2::making a copy}}.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.front: What is the key difference between copy semantics and move semantics?
cards.back: Copy semantics duplicates an object, leaving both the source and destination valid. Move semantics transfers ownership of resources from source to destination, leaving the source in a valid but unspecified state.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.explanation:
Move semantics is beneficial when:
  • Copying an object is expensive (large dynamic allocations)
  • The source object is a temporary that will be destroyed anyway
  • You need to transfer unique ownership of a resource
examples: std::unique_ptr<int> p2 = std::move(p1); // move, not copy
// p1 is now null, p2 owns the resource
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.text: C++11 introduced three smart pointer types: {{c1::std::unique_ptr}}, {{c2::std::shared_ptr}}, and {{c3::std::weak_ptr}}.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.front: What problems do smart pointers solve compared to raw pointers?
cards.back:
Smart pointers solve:
  • Memory leaks from forgetting to delete
  • Early returns or exceptions bypassing delete
  • Multiple or missing deletes
  • Automatic cleanup when going out of scope
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.answer: Memory leak. If condition is true, the function returns early without deleting ptr, causing the allocated memory to leak.
cards.explanation: Smart pointers would automatically clean up when going out of scope, preventing this leak.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.text: Smart pointers implement the {{c1::RAII}} (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) idiom, where resources are acquired in the {{c2::constructor}} and released in the {{c3::destructor}}.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.front: What should you use instead of std::auto_ptr in modern C++?
cards.back: std::unique_ptr. It properly implements move semantics and is the preferred smart pointer for single ownership in C++11 and later.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics

cards.text: Move semantics requires implementing a {{c1::move constructor}} and a {{c2::move assignment operator}} instead of using copy operations.
cards.source: 22.1 - Introduction to smart pointers and move semantics
